name: Blarney Castle
description: Famous for the Blarney Stone which supposedly gives the 'gift of the gab', Blarney Castle is one of Ireland's most visited attractions. Its poison garden and grounds are equally fascinating.
history: Blarney Castle is a medieval stronghold in Blarney, a town in Cork, Ireland. Though earlier fortifications were built on the same spot, the current keep was built by the MacCarthy of Muskerry dynasty, a cadet branch of the Kings of Desmond, and dates from 1446. The Blarney Stone is among the machicolations of the castle.

name: Ross Castle
description: A tower house on the shore of Lough Leane in Killarney National Park, Ross Castle was the stronghold of the O'Donoghue chieftains. It was one of the last places in Munster to fall to Cromwell's forces.
history: Ross Castle is a 15th-century tower house and keep on the edge of Lough Leane, in Killarney National Park, County Kerry, Ireland. It is the ancestral home of the Chiefs of the Clan O'Donoghue, later associated with the Brownes of Killarney.

name: Charles Fort
description: A massive star-shaped fort guarding Kinsale harbour, Charles Fort was built after the Williamite War. One of the largest military forts in Ireland, it saw action during the 1690 Siege of Kinsale.
history: Charles Hoy Fort was an American writer and researcher who specialized in anomalous phenomena. The terms "Fortean" and "Forteana" are sometimes used to characterize various such phenomena. Fort's books sold well and are still in print. His work continues to inspire admirers, who refer to themselves as "Forteans", and has influenced some aspects of science fiction.

name: Desmond Castle
description: A 15th-century tower house in Kinsale built by the Earls of Desmond, later used as a customs house and prison. It now houses the International Museum of Wine.
history: Desmond Castle may refer to:Desmond Castle (Adare) Desmond Castle (Askeaton) Desmond Castle (Kinsale) Desmond Hall and Castle, Newcastle West any other castle built or belonging to the Earls of Desmond

name: Skellig Michael
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site, this remote island monastery perches 200 metres above the Atlantic on a jagged rock pinnacle. Its beehive huts and oratories have survived 1,400 years of Atlantic storms.
history: Skellig Michael, also called Great Skellig, is a twin-pinnacled crag 11.6 kilometres (7.2 mi) west of the Iveragh Peninsula in County Kerry, Ireland. The island is named after the archangel Michael; while "Skellig" is derived from the Irish language word sceilig, meaning a splinter of stone. Its twin island, Little Skellig, is smaller and inaccessible. The two islands were formed c. 374–360 million years ago during a period of mountain formation, along with the local MacGillycuddy's Reeks mountain range. They were later separated from the mainland by rising water levels.

name: Askeaton Castle
description: Dramatic limestone ruins of a Desmond stronghold rising above the River Deel, its Great Hall one of the finest medieval banqueting chambers in Ireland.
history: Built by the Earls of Desmond in the 13th century, Askeaton became the chief seat of the Fitzgerald dynasty. The exquisite Banqueting Hall dates to the 15th century, featuring intricate carved windows. The castle was besieged multiple times during the Desmond Rebellions and finally fell to Cromwellian forces in 1652. The Franciscan friary adjacent was founded in 1389.

name: Carrigogunnell Castle
description: A dramatic castle ruin perched on a volcanic rock overlooking the Shannon estuary. Its O'Brien builders chose the site for its commanding views across Limerick and Clare.
history: Carrigogunnell Castle is a medieval Irish fortification near the village of Clarina, on the banks of the River Shannon in County Limerick.

name: Dunloe Castle
description: Ancient Anglo-Norman fortress guarding the fabled Gap of Dunloe, its weathered towers commanding views across Kerry's most dramatic mountain pass.
history: First recorded in 1215 when rebuilt by the son of Thomas Fitzgerald, this Anglo-Norman castle controlled the strategic river crossing to the Gap of Dunloe. It changed hands repeatedly between Gaelic and Norman lords, was partly demolished during the Cromwellian campaigns, and fell into dereliction. Rescued from collapse in 2013, it now stands within the grounds of the Dunloe Hotel.

name: Ballycarbery Castle
description: An ivy-covered McCarthy Mór castle overlooking Ballinskelligs Bay on the Ring of Kerry. Its atmospheric ruins stand in farmland with views toward the Skellig Islands.
history: Ballycarbery Castle is a castle 3 kilometres (2 mi) from Cahersiveen, County Kerry, Ireland. The castle is high on a grass hill facing the sea and is a short distance from Cahergall Fort and Leacanabuile Fort.

name: Barryscourt Castle
description: A well-restored tower house of the Barry family near Carrigtwohill, with three corner towers and a bawn wall. Its restored garden recreates a 16th-century Irish herb garden.
history: Barryscourt Castle is a castle located in eastern County Cork in southern Ireland, close to the town of Carrigtwohill.

name: Kilcrea Friary
description: A Franciscan friary founded by Cormac Láidir McCarthy in 1465, remarkably well preserved with an intact tower. The adjacent Kilcrea Castle tower house still stands nearby.
history: Kilcrea Friary is a ruined medieval abbey located near Ovens, County Cork, Ireland. Both the friary and Kilcrea Castle, located in ruin to the west, were built by Observant Franciscans in the mid 15th century under the invitation of Cormac Láidir MacCarthy, Lord of Muskerry, as protection from English troops.

name: Timoleague Abbey
description: A Franciscan friary beautifully situated on the edge of a tidal inlet in West Cork. It was one of the largest friaries in Ireland and its extensive ruins include a fine cloister.
history: Timoleague Friary, also known as Timoleague Abbey, is a ruined medieval Franciscan friary in Timoleague, County Cork, Ireland, on the banks of the Argideen River overlooking Courtmacsherry Bay. It was built on the site of an early Christian monastic site founded by Saint Molaga, from whom the town of Timoleague derives its name. The present remains date from roughly the turn of the fourteenth century and were burnt down by British forces in the mid-seventeenth century, at which point it was an important ecclesiastical centre that engaged in significant trade with Spain.

name: Kanturk Castle
description: An unfinished Elizabethan-style fortified house built by MacDonagh McCarthy around 1609. The Privy Council ordered construction halted, fearing it was too grand for an Irish lord.
history: Kanturk is a town in the north west of County Cork, Ireland. It is situated at the confluence of the Allua (Allow) and Dallow (Dalua) rivers, which stream further on as tributaries to the River Blackwater. It is about 50 kilometres from Cork and Limerick, and lies just north of the main N72 road, 15 km from Mallow and about 40 km from Killarney. Kanturk is within the Cork North-West Dáil constituency.

name: Drombeg Stone Circle
description: A perfectly preserved Bronze Age stone circle perched above the wild West Cork coast — where 17 ancient stones still mark the winter solstice sunset with eerie precision.
history: Dating from c.1100-800 BC, this recumbent stone circle of 17 closely-spaced sandstone pillars (13 surviving) is aligned with the winter solstice sunset. Excavated in 1958, revealing cremated remains of an adolescent in a pot at the centre. Nearby are the remains of a fulacht fiadh (ancient cooking pit) and two roundhouses. Sometimes called 'The Druid's Altar'. Free access.

name: Glenstal Castle
description: A romantic Norman Revival castle set in ancient woodland, now home to a Benedictine monastery whose monks chant amid Victorian fantasy architecture.
history: Built between 1837 and 1839 by the Barrington family in an extravagant Norman Revival style, Glenstal Castle was designed by William Bardwell. The estate was purchased by the Benedictine order in 1927 and now houses Glenstal Abbey, a monastery and prestigious boys' school. The castle features fine medieval-inspired stonework and sits in 500 acres of parkland.

name: Kilcoe Castle
description: A dramatically restored tower house on a tidal island in Roaringwater Bay, famously owned and brought back to life by actor Jeremy Irons.
history: Built in the 15th century by the McCarthy clan, Kilcoe Castle sits on a rocky tidal island in Roaringwater Bay, West Cork. It passed through several families before falling into ruin. In 1998, actor Jeremy Irons purchased and meticulously restored the castle, controversially painting it white initially before the traditional grey stone was restored. The castle is not open to the public.

name: Dunboy Castle
description: Tragic ruins of the O'Sullivan Bere stronghold on the Beara Peninsula, site of one of the last stands against Elizabethan conquest.
history: Dunboy Castle near Castletownbere was the seat of the O'Sullivan Bere clan. In 1602, during the Nine Years' War, it was besieged and destroyed by English forces under Sir George Carew after a ferocious defence. Donal Cam O'Sullivan Bere then led his legendary march to Leitrim. A later Puxley mansion was built nearby and later destroyed during the War of Independence.

name: Mallow Castle
description: Ivy-draped ruins of a grand Elizabethan fortified house in the heart of Mallow, surrounded by a deer park where herds still graze beneath ancient oaks.
history: Built around 1585 by Sir Thomas Norreys, Lord President of Munster, Mallow Castle was one of the finest Elizabethan fortified houses in Ireland. The three-storey mansion replaced an earlier Desmond stronghold. It was badly damaged during the Cromwellian wars. A Georgian house was built alongside in the 18th century, and the grounds remain a public park with a resident deer herd.

name: Macroom Castle
description: Gateway to West Cork's wild interior, this medieval castle's surviving archway frames the bustling market town that grew in its protective shadow.
history: Originally built by the McCarthy clan in the medieval period, Macroom Castle was later held by the Muskerry branch of the McCarthys. It was granted to Admiral Sir William Penn (father of William Penn of Pennsylvania fame) during the Cromwellian settlement. The castle was largely destroyed by fire in 1922 during the Civil War, but the impressive gateway and some walls remain.

name: Minard Castle
description: A spectacularly ruined fortress on a wild Dingle Peninsula beach, its shattered walls testimony to Cromwell's devastating bombardment.
history: Built by the Knight of Kerry (FitzGerald family) in the 16th century, Minard Castle stands on a storm beach on the Dingle Peninsula. During the Cromwellian wars in 1650, the castle was besieged and its garrison massacred. The castle was deliberately demolished afterward and now stands as a dramatic ruin surrounded by massive storm-tossed boulders on the Kerry coast.

name: Listowel Castle
description: A proud Norman fortress standing watch over the literary capital of Kerry, its twin towers a gateway to centuries of Munster history and rebellion.
history: Built by the Anglo-Norman Fitzmaurice family (Lords of Kerry) in the 15th century, Listowel Castle was the last castle in Munster to hold out against the Elizabethan conquest, finally surrendering in 1600 after a lengthy siege. The garrison was executed. The impressive twin towers and connecting wall have been restored and the castle is now open to the public.

name: Carrigafoyle Castle
description: A towering fortress on the Shannon Estuary, its five-storey tower offering staggering views and a visceral sense of the power struggles that ravaged Munster.
history: Built in the late 15th century by the O'Connor Kerry clan, Carrigafoyle Castle was one of the principal fortresses of the Shannon Estuary. In 1580 it was besieged by Sir William Pelham's Elizabethan forces with cannon fire—the first recorded use of artillery against an Irish castle. The garrison of 50 was massacred. The castle's impressive height and strategic position remain striking.

name: Dromore Castle (Limerick)
description: A flamboyant Gothic Revival fantasy rising from the Limerick countryside, its turrets and towers designed by the great Edward William Godwin.
history: Built between 1867 and 1873 for the 3rd Earl of Limerick by architect E.W. Godwin (famous for his Aesthetic Movement designs), Dromore Castle is a spectacular Gothic Revival confection. The castle was abandoned in the 1950s when the family could no longer afford its upkeep. It stands as one of Ireland's most dramatic Victorian castle ruins.

name: Kilbolane Castle
description: A massive medieval motte-and-castle ruin crowning a hill in north Cork, its sprawling earthworks testimony to centuries of frontier warfare.
history: Kilbolane Castle in County Cork dates from the 13th century and was built by the de Barry family. The large motte and ruined tower house occupy a commanding hilltop position. It changed hands between Norman and Gaelic lords numerous times and was involved in the Desmond Rebellions. The earthworks and tower remains offer wide views across the Blackwater valley.

name: Gallarus Castle
description: A fine 15th-century tower house standing near the famous Gallarus Oratory on the Dingle Peninsula, bridging a thousand years of Kerry heritage.
history: Built by the Knight of Kerry (FitzGerald family) in the 15th century, Gallarus Castle is a well-preserved tower house near the renowned Gallarus Oratory, an early Christian dry-stone church dating to perhaps the 8th century. The castle served as a FitzGerald stronghold on the Dingle Peninsula and retains its four storeys and much of its original fabric.

name: Rahinnane Castle
description: A ruined FitzGerald tower house on the Dingle Peninsula, perched on an ancient ringfort with spectacular views across Ventry Harbour to the Atlantic.
history: Built on the site of a much older ringfort, Rahinnane Castle was a FitzGerald (Knight of Kerry) stronghold on the Dingle Peninsula. The tower house dates to the 15th century and commands views over Ventry Harbour. The underlying ringfort suggests the site's strategic importance stretching back into pre-Norman times.

name: Killaha Castle
description: A small FitzGerald tower house in the Kerry countryside near Kenmare, its modest proportions belying its significance in the intricate web of Desmond power.
history: Killaha Castle is a tower house associated with the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond, located near Kenmare in County Kerry. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the Desmond lordship that controlled much of Munster. After the Desmond Rebellions and the subsequent confiscation of lands, the castle fell into decay.

name: Dunkerron Castle
description: Atmospheric ruins of an O'Sullivan Mor stronghold overlooking the Kenmare estuary, where chieftains once commanded the wild reaches of south Kerry.
history: Dunkerron Castle near Kenmare was the principal stronghold of the O'Sullivan Mor clan, chiefs of the Beara and Bantry region. The medieval castle controlled access to the Kenmare River estuary. The O'Sullivans were major Gaelic lords who resisted English expansion until the 17th century. The castle ruins stand in a picturesque setting overlooking Kenmare Bay.

name: Lixnaw Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of the Fitzmaurice Lords of Kerry's ancestral stronghold, now absorbed into the quiet north Kerry village that was once a seat of power.
history: Lixnaw was the ancestral seat of the Fitzmaurice family, Lords of Kerry, from the 13th century. The castle served as their chief residence and the village developed around it. The Fitzmaurices played significant roles in the Desmond Rebellions. Little of the original castle remains today, with fragments incorporated into later buildings in the village.

name: Belvelly Castle
description: A fine medieval tower house guarding the narrow strait between Great Island and the mainland, the gateway to Cork's historic harbour.
history: Belvelly Castle dates to the 15th century and guards the narrow channel between Cobh's Great Island and the mainland in Cork Harbour. It was held by the Hodnett family and later the Barrymore Barrys. The four-storey tower house has been restored and is one of the few tower houses in the Cork harbour area to survive substantially intact.

name: Ballinacarriga Castle
description: A beautifully situated tower house rising above a lake in West Cork, its rare sheela-na-gig carving adding a touch of medieval mystery.
history: Built around 1585 by Randal Hurley (O'Hurley), Ballinacarriga Castle is a well-preserved tower house in West Cork. The castle features a notable sheela-na-gig carving over its entrance, one of the best-preserved examples in Ireland. The five-storey tower overlooks a small lake and retains fine architectural details including carved window surrounds.

name: Castletownshend Castle
description: The historic Townshend family seat overlooking the picturesque harbour village of Castletownshend, one of West Cork's most charming settlements.
history: Castletownshend has been the seat of the Townshend family since the 17th century plantation era. The village, perched on a steep hill above the harbour, was home to authors Edith Somerville and Martin Ross, famous for their 'Irish R.M.' stories. The castle and associated buildings reflect the Ascendancy world of West Cork's Anglo-Irish families.

name: Carriganass Castle
description: A dramatic O'Sullivan Bere tower house at the head of Bantry Bay, steeped in legends of the Elizabethan wars and the flight of the Gaelic lords.
history: Built by the O'Sullivan Bere clan in the 16th century, Carriganass Castle near Kealkill in West Cork was a key stronghold during the Elizabethan conquest of Munster. It was at Carriganass that Donal Cam O'Sullivan Bere planned his resistance against the English. The castle was captured and partially destroyed during the Nine Years' War. Restored in recent years, it stands as a memorial to Gaelic resistance.

name: Lohort Castle
description: A magnificent 15th-century tower house in the Blackwater Valley, dramatically enlarged in the 19th century into an imposing castellated mansion.
history: Originally built by the Roche family in the 15th century, Lohort Castle in north Cork was extensively remodelled and enlarged in the 19th century. The castle stands in a fine estate along the Blackwater Valley. It was home to several prominent families including the Percevals. The combination of medieval tower and Victorian additions creates an impressive silhouette in the lush Blackwater landscape.

name: Monkstown Castle
description: A striking Elizabethan fortified house overlooking Cork Harbour, its distinctive profile visible from the city's harbour ferries and waterfront walks.
history: Built around 1636 by the Archdeacon family, Monkstown Castle is a well-preserved fortified house overlooking Cork Harbour. The substantial building features corbelled turrets, mullioned windows, and an impressive entrance. Its prominent position above the harbour made it a landmark for centuries of shipping entering Cork. The castle has undergone various restorations.

name: Carrigaphooca Castle
description: The 'Rock of the Fairy' — a dramatic McCarthy tower house perched on a massive glacial boulder beside the road to Macroom, steeped in folklore and shadow.
history: Built by the McCarthy clan in the 15th century, Carrigaphooca (Rock of the Fairy) Castle sits atop a huge glacial erratic boulder along the road between Cork and Macroom. Legend says the rock was placed by a pooka (fairy spirit). The tower house commanded the route into West Cork and was a McCarthy stronghold until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Ballincollig Castle
description: Substantial medieval remains in the heart of Cork's largest suburb, a surprising survivor of Norman power amid modern housing estates and shopping centres.
history: Built by the de Cogan family in the 13th century and later held by the Barretts and the Earls of Desmond, Ballincollig Castle is a significant Norman ruin now surrounded by Cork's suburban sprawl. The castle features a large rectangular tower and curtain wall. It played a role in the Desmond Rebellions before passing to English settlers. Now maintained as a local heritage site.

name: Dundanion Castle
description: A small but atmospheric medieval tower in the Cork suburb of Blackrock, its ancient stones now surrounded by parkland and sea views.
history: Dundanion Castle in Blackrock, Cork, is a medieval tower house that once overlooked the southern channel of Cork Harbour. The compact structure dates to the medieval period and was associated with various local families. Now preserved in a public park setting, it provides an unexpected medieval encounter in suburban Cork.

name: Glin Castle
description: The ancestral seat of the Knights of Glin on the Shannon Estuary—a Georgian Gothic castle that was home to the FitzGerald dynasty for over 700 years.
history: Glin Castle has been the seat of the FitzGerald Knights of Glin for over 700 years. The current Gothic castle dates largely to the 1780s with later additions. The last Knight of Glin, Desmond FitzGerald, was a noted art historian and Christie's chairman who died in 2011. The castle and its beautiful gardens overlooking the Shannon Estuary have operated as a luxury accommodation venue.

name: Castle Oliver
description: A fantastical Victorian Gothic castle in the Limerick hills, reputedly the birthplace of the legendary Lola Montez and a masterpiece of theatrical architecture.
history: Built between 1845 and 1852 by the Gascoigne-Oliver family in an extravagant Gothic Revival style, Castle Oliver in County Limerick is a spectacular Victorian confection. The estate claims connection to Lola Montez (Eliza Gilbert), the dancer who became mistress of King Ludwig I of Bavaria. The castle was derelict for decades before being restored and is now available for exclusive hire.

name: Shanid Castle
description: The ancient cradle of the Desmond FitzGeralds, whose war cry 'Shanid abú!' echoed across Munster for three centuries of rebellion and conquest.
history: Shanid Castle near Shanagolden is the ancestral seat of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond, who took the war cry 'Shanid abú!' (Shanid forever!) from this fortress. The motte and castle date to the early 13th century and represent one of the earliest Norman strongholds in Munster. The Desmonds controlled much of south-west Ireland from here until the Desmond Rebellions of the 16th century.

name: Ballingarry Castle (Limerick)
description: A medieval tower in the Limerick countryside, linked to the turbulent Desmond dynasty and the wars that ravaged Munster.
history: Ballingarry Castle in County Limerick is a medieval tower house associated with the Desmond FitzGeralds and later families. The castle dates to the 15th century and stands in the agricultural heartland of west Limerick. It was part of the wider network of fortifications that controlled the Desmond lordship.

name: Muckross Friary
description: A hauntingly intact Franciscan friary in Killarney National Park — where an ancient yew tree grows in the cloister and Ireland's last Gaelic poets lie buried.
history: Founded in 1448 for the Observantine Franciscans by Donal McCarthy Mor. Previously known as the Friary of Irrelagh. Remarkably well-preserved despite suppression and occupation by Protestant forces. The broad-canopied yew tree in the cloister is believed to be as old as the friary itself. Burial place of the great Gaelic poets Aodhagán Ó Rathaille and Eoghan Rua Ó Súilleabháin.

name: Buttevant Friary
description: Substantial Franciscan friary ruins in the north Cork town that gave the world the word 'steeplechase,' its medieval walls still standing amid the bustle of daily life.
history: Founded in 1251 by David Óg Barry, Buttevant Friary (Ballybeg Abbey) is a substantial Franciscan foundation in north Cork. The town of Buttevant is said to have given its name to the steeplechase—a race between the steeples of Buttevant and Doneraile churches in 1752. The friary features a fine church, cloister, and domestic buildings.

name: Carrigadrohid Castle
description: A dramatic castle ruin on a rocky island in the River Lee, where a Catholic bishop was hanged from the walls during the Cromwellian wars.
history: Carrigadrohid Castle stands on a rock in the River Lee west of Cork city. Built by the McCarthy clan in the medieval period, it gained infamy during the Cromwellian wars when Bishop Boetius MacEgan was captured while trying to rally Confederate forces and hanged from the castle walls in 1650. The castle ruins are accessible via a bridge and create a dramatic riverside scene.

name: Castledonovan
description: A remote McCarthy tower house in the wild West Cork hills, its isolated setting among bog and mountain lending it an air of frontier defiance.
history: Castledonovan is a tower house built by the McCarthy clan in the 15th century in a remote location in West Cork. The castle was a stronghold of the McCarthy Reagh (southern McCarthy) branch. Its isolated hillside setting, surrounded by bog and rough grazing, reflects the harsh conditions of life in medieval West Cork.

name: Dunmanus Castle
description: A lonely O'Mahony tower on the wild shores of Dunmanus Bay, where the Mizen Peninsula's dramatic coastline meets the deep Atlantic.
history: Dunmanus Castle is an O'Mahony tower house on the shore of Dunmanus Bay on the Mizen Peninsula in West Cork. The castle dates to the 15th century and controlled access to the bay. The O'Mahonys held extensive territory along the West Cork coast. The castle's remote setting on one of Ireland's least-visited peninsulas adds to its atmosphere.

name: Rossbrin Castle
description: A small O'Mahony tower house overlooking Roaringwater Bay, where seals bask on rocks below walls that once watched for approaching Viking longships.
history: Rossbrin Castle near Schull in West Cork is a small tower house built by the O'Mahony clan. The castle overlooks Roaringwater Bay with views to the offshore islands. The O'Mahonys controlled much of the coastline between Baltimore and Bantry. The castle's compact scale and coastal setting make it characteristic of West Cork's maritime heritage.

name: Cloghane Castle
description: Ruins in the remote Dingle Peninsula village of Cloghane, nestled beneath the towering bulk of Mount Brandon and the wild Kerry mountains.
history: Cloghane Castle is a medieval fortification in the village of Cloghane on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry. The village sits beneath Mount Brandon, Ireland's second-highest mountain and an important pilgrimage site associated with St Brendan the Navigator. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Togher Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Cork countryside, one of the many medieval fortifications that dotted the fertile Blackwater and Lee valleys.
history: Togher Castle is a medieval tower house in County Cork. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of numerous tower houses in the productive agricultural lands of central Cork. The tower served as a fortified residence for a local family.

name: Castlemagner Castle
description: A medieval stronghold in the Blackwater Valley of north Cork, associated with the powerful Roche and Barry families of medieval Munster.
history: Castlemagner Castle in north Cork dates to the medieval period and was held by various Anglo-Norman families including the Roches and Barrys. The castle controlled territory in the fertile Blackwater Valley. The area was part of the complex patchwork of Norman and Gaelic lordships in medieval Munster.

name: Liscarroll Castle
description: One of the largest and most impressive Norman castles in Ireland, its massive curtain walls enclosing a vast area in the north Cork countryside.
history: Liscarroll Castle in north Cork is one of the largest Norman castles in Ireland, with massive curtain walls and corner towers enclosing a large rectangular area. Built in the 13th century, possibly by the Barry family, the castle was a major military stronghold. It was the site of a significant battle in 1642 during the Confederate Wars when Garret Barry defeated English forces.

name: Castlepook Castle
description: Ruins near the famous Castlepook Cave, one of Ireland's most important archaeological sites where Ice Age bear bones were discovered.
history: Castlepook Castle in north Cork stands near Castlepook Cave, where excavations revealed the bones of brown bears, arctic foxes, and other Ice Age fauna dating back 40,000 years. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortifications in the Blackwater Valley region.

name: Drishane Castle
description: A Victorian castellated mansion in Millstreet, north Cork, set in extensive grounds that now host the prestigious Millstreet International Horse Show.
history: Drishane Castle in Millstreet, County Cork, is a castellated Victorian mansion built on the site of an earlier McCarthy stronghold. The estate, now owned by Noel C. Duggan, hosts the Millstreet International Horse Show and has hosted the Eurovision Song Contest in 1993. The grounds cover over 400 acres of parkland.

name: Dromaneen Castle
description: A fine tower house in the rich farmland of north Cork, associated with the storied Nagle family who shaped Irish history.
history: Dromaneen Castle near Mallow in north Cork is a tower house associated with the Nagle family. Nano Nagle, founder of the Presentation Sisters and a pioneer of Catholic education in penal Ireland, was connected to this area. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the fertile Blackwater Valley farmland.

name: Castlelyons Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the pretty village of Castlelyons, where the powerful Barrymore Barrys once held sway over a wide swath of east Cork.
history: Castlelyons was the seat of the Barry family, Viscounts Barrymore, one of the most powerful Anglo-Norman families in Cork. The castle dates to the medieval period and the village retains a planned estate character. The nearby Barryscourt Castle in Carrigtwohill is a better-preserved Barry stronghold. A fine Carmelite friary also stands in the village.

name: Conna Castle
description: A tall, dramatic FitzGerald tower on the banks of the Bride River, its dark stone walls and strategic position reflecting centuries of Desmond power.
history: Conna Castle in east Cork is a tall tower house on the River Bride, built by the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond in the 15th century. The castle was a significant Desmond stronghold. Thomas FitzGerald, the last Earl of Desmond, was captured and killed near here in 1583, effectively ending the Desmond Rebellions. The imposing ruins overlook the river.

name: Ballymalis Castle
description: A tower house ruin near Killorglin in Kerry, standing in the shadow of the Reeks mountains in one of Ireland's most scenic landscapes.
history: Ballymalis Castle is a medieval tower house near Killorglin, County Kerry. Built in the 15th or 16th century, likely by the McCarthy or FitzGerald families, the castle stands in the scenic area between Killorglin and the MacGillycuddy's Reeks mountain range. The tower is in a ruinous state but retains some structural features.

name: Castleisland Castle
description: The historic heart of the Kerry town that bears its name, once a major FitzGerald stronghold commanding the route into the Dingle Peninsula.
history: Castleisland Castle was originally built by Geoffrey de Marisco in the 13th century and later became a stronghold of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond. The town grew around the castle and became an important market centre. The castle was destroyed during the Desmond Rebellions and again in the Cromwellian period. Fragments remain in the town centre.

name: Ballyheigue Castle
description: Romantic ruins of a Crosby family mansion above the long sandy beach of Ballyheigue on Kerry's north coast.
history: Ballyheigue Castle was the seat of the Crosby family in north Kerry. The castle estate overlooked one of Kerry's finest beaches. The original medieval castle was replaced by a substantial mansion that fell into ruin. The atmospheric remains stand above the sweeping bay with views to Kerry Head.

name: Fenit Castle
description: Coastal fortification remains at the harbour of Fenit, departure point for St Brendan's legendary voyage across the Atlantic.
history: Fenit in County Kerry is traditionally the birthplace and departure point of St Brendan the Navigator, who is said to have sailed to North America in the 6th century. The castle or fortification at Fenit dates to the medieval period and guarded the harbour. A modern statue of St Brendan commemorates the village's most famous son.

name: Parkavonear Castle
description: A romantically ruined tower house beside the lakes of Killarney, its ivy-clad walls reflecting in the still waters of the Aghadoe landscape.
history: Parkavonear Castle near Aghadoe outside Killarney is a medieval tower house in a picturesque lakeside setting. The castle was held by various families including the McCarthys and later the Herberts. Its position near the famous lakes of Killarney and the ancient church of Aghadoe makes it part of a rich heritage landscape.

name: Castlegregory Tower
description: A small tower in the Dingle Peninsula village of Castlegregory, gateway to the wild Maharees Peninsula and its spectacular beaches.
history: Castlegregory takes its name from Gregory Hoare's castle, built in the medieval period. The village sits at the base of the Maharees Peninsula, a sand spit with beautiful beaches and the historically significant Brandon Creek. The castle remains are modest but the village's heritage as a medieval settlement endures.

name: Castlemaine Castle
description: Scant but historically significant remains near the head of Castlemaine Harbour, where the River Maine meets the sea in the shadow of the Slieve Mish mountains.
history: Castlemaine Castle stood near the head of Castlemaine Harbour in Kerry, controlling the junction of several routes into the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas. The castle dates to the medieval period and was an important strategic position. The Castlemaine harbour area was traditionally a significant trading and fishing centre.

name: Duagh Castle
description: A medieval tower ruin in the quiet Kerry village of Duagh, birthplace of the great playwright and writer John B. Keane.
history: Duagh Castle is a medieval tower house in the north Kerry village of Duagh. The village is best known as the birthplace of John B. Keane, one of Ireland's most celebrated playwrights, author of 'The Field' and 'Sive.' The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Castle Matrix
description: A beautifully restored FitzGerald tower house near Rathkeale, where Walter Raleigh and Edmund Spenser once dined and the potato was reputedly first planted in Ireland.
history: Castle Matrix near Rathkeale, County Limerick, dates to 1440 and was built by the 7th Earl of Desmond. The castle claims to be where Sir Walter Raleigh met Edmund Spenser and where the potato was first planted in Ireland. Beautifully restored in the 20th century, it housed an important collection of rare books and manuscripts.

name: Ballygrennan Castle
description: A tower house near Limerick city, its medieval walls now surrounded by the expanding suburbs of Ireland's third-largest city.
history: Ballygrennan Castle is a medieval tower house on the outskirts of Limerick city. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of several tower houses that guarded the approaches to the city. As Limerick has expanded, the castle has become an urban heritage site amid modern development.

name: Shanpallas Castle
description: A quiet tower house in the Limerick countryside, one of many medieval fortifications scattered across the rich lands of the old Desmond lordship.
history: Shanpallas Castle is a medieval tower house in County Limerick. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was part of the extensive network of fortified residences in the Desmond lordship. The tower stands in agricultural land in the fertile Limerick plain.

name: Fanningstown Castle
description: A restored medieval castle near Adare, offering atmospheric self-catering accommodation within authentic 15th-century walls.
history: Fanningstown Castle near Adare in County Limerick is a 15th-century tower house that has been restored and converted into luxury self-catering accommodation. The castle retains many original features and offers guests an authentic medieval castle experience in one of Ireland's prettiest villages. Nearby Adare is famous for its thatched cottages and medieval religious houses.

name: Newcastle West Castle
description: Impressive Desmond castle remains in the heart of Newcastle West, featuring two remarkable medieval halls—the finest secular medieval buildings in Limerick.
history: Newcastle West Castle was a principal seat of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond. The castle complex includes Desmond Hall, a magnificent 15th-century banqueting hall, and the earlier Hall of the Asking. These are among the finest medieval secular buildings in Ireland. The castle was confiscated after the Desmond Rebellions and later partly demolished.

name: Cloyne Round Tower
description: A fine early Christian round tower in the east Cork cathedral town of Cloyne, once seat of the famous philosopher Bishop George Berkeley.
history: The Round Tower at Cloyne, County Cork, stands beside the medieval cathedral and dates to the 10th-11th century. Cloyne was an important early Christian monastic settlement. The town's most famous bishop was George Berkeley (1685-1753), the philosopher after whom Berkeley, California, is named. The tower is well-preserved and forms part of an attractive heritage streetscape.

name: Kilcolman Castle
description: The haunting ruin where Edmund Spenser wrote 'The Faerie Queene'—one of the greatest works in the English language, composed in the wilds of Cork.
history: Kilcolman Castle near Doneraile in north Cork was the home of the poet Edmund Spenser, who wrote much of 'The Faerie Queene' here in the 1580s-90s. Spenser received the castle as part of the Munster Plantation. The castle was burned in the 1598 Munster Rebellion and Spenser fled to London, where he died shortly after. The evocative ruins overlook a small lake.

name: Castletownroche Castle
description: Castle remains in the pretty Cork village of Castletownroche, strategically sited above the River Awbeg in the Blackwater Valley.
history: Castletownroche Castle in County Cork stands above the River Awbeg (a tributary of the Blackwater) in the village of Castletownroche. The castle was built by the Roche family, a powerful Anglo-Norman dynasty in Cork. The nearby Annes Grove Gardens are a celebrated woodland garden open to visitors.

name: Ightermurragh Castle
description: A fine tower house near the east Cork coast, standing amid the farmland between Castlemartyr and Ladysbridge.
history: Ightermurragh Castle in east Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and is one of several tower houses in the east Cork countryside. The area is rich in heritage, with the nearby medieval town of Cloyne and various Anglo-Norman castles and churches.

name: Kilbrittain Castle
description: A dramatic medieval tower house perched above the village of Kilbrittain in west Cork, with views stretching to the Celtic Sea.
history: Kilbrittain Castle in west Cork is a substantial tower house dating to the medieval period. The castle was associated with the de Courcey family and later the McCarthy clan. It commands an elevated position above the village with views toward the sea. The castle has been partially restored.

name: Rostellan Castle
description: Atmospheric ruins of an 18th-century mansion on the shores of Cork Harbour, its grounds once the most celebrated gardens in Munster.
history: Rostellan Castle near Whitegate in east Cork was an elaborate 18th-century mansion on the shores of Cork Harbour. The estate was famous for its extensive gardens and ornamental grounds. The house fell into ruin in the 20th century. The atmospheric shell stands near the shores of the harbour.

name: Castle Salem
description: A rare intact Elizabethan fortified house in West Cork, privately restored and offering an authentic glimpse into 16th-century Irish life.
history: Castle Salem near Rosscarbery in West Cork is an Elizabethan fortified house dating to the late 16th century. The building was constructed during the Munster Plantation and retains many original features. It has been carefully restored by its owners and occasionally opens to visitors, offering a rare experience of an authentic Elizabethan interior in Ireland.

name: Castlemartyr Castle
description: Medieval castle ruins in the east Cork village of Castlemartyr, where a Desmond stronghold now forms the centrepiece of a luxury resort.
history: Castlemartyr Castle in east Cork was a FitzGerald (Desmond) stronghold dating to the medieval period. The castle and estate were later developed into a grand country house. The entire estate was converted into the Castlemartyr Resort, a luxury hotel. The medieval ruins are preserved within the resort grounds.

name: Aghadoe Castle
description: Medieval ruins overlooking the glorious Lakes of Killarney, combining a cathedral, round tower, and castle in one of Kerry's most scenic viewpoints.
history: Aghadoe near Killarney, County Kerry, is a remarkable heritage site combining a 12th-century cathedral, round tower stump, and 13th-century castle ruin. The site commands one of the finest views of the Lakes of Killarney and the surrounding mountains. The cathedral was an important medieval see and the castle a Norman fortification.

name: Rattoo Round Tower
description: A fine round tower and medieval church ruin in north Kerry, one of the best-preserved early Christian monuments in Munster.
history: Rattoo Round Tower near Ballyduff in County Kerry stands 28 metres tall and dates to the 11th or 12th century. It is one of the best-preserved round towers in Munster. The adjacent church ruins and graveyard complete an atmospheric early Christian site. The tower retains its conical cap and the characteristic raised doorway.

name: Muckross House
description: A magnificent Victorian mansion at the heart of Killarney National Park, where Queen Victoria's visit in 1861 nearly bankrupted the Herbert family.
history: Muckross House, built 1839-1843 for Henry Arthur Herbert, is a Tudor Revival mansion set in the spectacular scenery of Killarney National Park. Queen Victoria visited in 1861, and the preparations for her visit were so lavish they contributed to the Herbert family's bankruptcy. The house and its traditional farms are now a major visitor attraction managed by the OPW.

name: Derrynane House
description: The ancestral home of Daniel O'Connell, 'The Liberator,' set in the stunning coastal scenery of the Iveragh Peninsula in County Kerry.
history: Derrynane House near Caherdaniel, County Kerry, was the home of Daniel O'Connell (1775-1847), who won Catholic Emancipation in 1829—one of the greatest political achievements of the 19th century. The house is now a museum dedicated to O'Connell's life and the struggle for Catholic rights. The grounds include beautiful gardens and access to Derrynane Beach.

name: Cahergall Stone Fort
description: A superbly preserved Iron Age stone fort on the Ring of Kerry, its massive drystone walls and beehive huts offering a vivid window into prehistoric Irish life.
history: Cahergall Stone Fort near Cahersiveen, County Kerry, is a spectacularly well-preserved cashel (stone ring fort) dating to around 600 AD. The massive drystone walls stand up to 5 metres high, and the fort contains the remains of a rectangular house and a clochán (beehive hut). Cahergall is one of the most impressive accessible stone forts in Ireland.

name: Leacanabuaile Fort
description: A beautifully restored stone ringfort near Cahersiveen, revealing the domestic arrangements of early medieval Irish farm life in remarkable detail.
history: Leacanabuaile Stone Fort near Cahersiveen, County Kerry, was excavated in 1939 and carefully restored. The cashel dates to the 9th-10th century and contained the foundations of houses and a souterrain. The excavation revealed artefacts including bronze pins, iron knives, and spinning whorls, illuminating daily life in early medieval Kerry.

name: Mountlong Castle
description: A substantial fortified house in the Waterford countryside, an unusually well-preserved example of early 17th-century plantation architecture.
history: Mountlong Castle near Dungarvan, County Waterford, is a fortified house dating to the early 17th century plantation period. The substantial building features fine details and represents the transition from medieval castle to Renaissance residence. The castle was associated with the Long family.

name: Ballybeg Priory
description: Well-preserved Augustinian priory ruins near Buttevant in north Cork, with an imposing tower and extensive medieval domestic buildings.
history: Ballybeg Priory near Buttevant, County Cork, was an Augustinian foundation dating to the 13th century. The priory is remarkably well preserved, with substantial remains of the church, tower, domestic ranges, and enclosure walls. It is one of the best-preserved medieval monastic sites in north Cork.

name: Bridgetown Priory
description: Extensive Augustinian priory ruins beside the River Anner in south Tipperary, one of the largest and most atmospheric monastic complexes in the southeast.
history: Bridgetown Priory near Castlewarren in County Kilkenny (bordering Tipperary) was an Augustinian foundation established in the 13th century. The extensive ruins include a large church, cloister, tower, and domestic buildings spread across a riverside site. The priory is one of the largest medieval monastic complexes in the southeast.

name: Ballyclogh Castle
description: A well-preserved tower house in the north Cork countryside, standing proudly amid the rich farmland of the Blackwater Valley.
history: Ballyclogh Castle in north Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house in the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century and retains good structural integrity. The Blackwater Valley is one of Ireland's richest agricultural areas and was densely settled during the medieval period.

name: Ardtully Castle
description: Ruins of a McCarthy stronghold near Kenmare in Kerry, in the wild landscape where the Roughty River flows toward Kenmare Bay.
history: Ardtully Castle near Kenmare, County Kerry, was a McCarthy Mor stronghold. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled territory along the Roughty River near Kenmare Bay. The McCarthys were one of the great Gaelic families of Munster.

name: Killagha Abbey
description: Augustinian abbey ruins near Milltown in Kerry, a tranquil monastic site in the gentle landscape between the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas.
history: Killagha Abbey near Milltown, County Kerry, was an Augustinian priory founded in the 13th century. The ruins include church remains with fine architectural details. The abbey sits in the quiet countryside between the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas.

name: Lislaughtin Abbey
description: A Franciscan friary ruin near Ballylongford in north Kerry, featuring one of the finest surviving medieval processional crosses in Ireland.
history: Lislaughtin Abbey near Ballylongford, County Kerry, was a Franciscan friary founded in 1464. The friary is notable for having preserved a remarkable medieval processional cross, now in the National Museum. The atmospheric ruins stand in the quiet north Kerry countryside.

name: Adare Desmond Castle
description: A large medieval castle on the banks of the River Maigue in Ireland's prettiest village, overlooking the famous thatched cottages and medieval churches of Adare.
history: The Desmond Castle in Adare, County Limerick, was built by the Earls of Kildare (Fitzgerald family) in the 13th century on the banks of the River Maigue. The substantial ruins include a great hall, two towers, and extensive curtain walls. The castle is accessible via the Adare Heritage Centre. Adare itself is famous for its picturesque thatched cottages and three medieval religious houses.

name: Castle Bernard
description: The spectacular roofless shell of a grand Georgian mansion in Bandon, burned during the War of Independence in one of Cork's most dramatic Big House destructions.
history: Castle Bernard near Bandon, County Cork, was the seat of the Earls of Bandon. The grand Georgian mansion was burned by the IRA in June 1921 during the War of Independence, with the Earl's wife narrowly escaping. The impressive shell, with its fine stonework and soaring chimney stacks, is one of Cork's most dramatic Big House ruins.

name: Sherkin Island Abbey
description: Franciscan friary ruins on the charming island of Sherkin in Roaringwater Bay, where medieval monks chose one of West Cork's most idyllic island settings.
history: Sherkin Island Abbey was a Franciscan friary founded in 1460 by the O'Driscoll family on Sherkin Island in Roaringwater Bay, West Cork. The friary was sacked by Waterford forces in 1537. The atmospheric ruins stand near the island's ferry pier. Sherkin Island is a popular day trip from Baltimore, with beautiful beaches and a marine research station.

name: Bantry House
description: A Georgian jewel overlooking Bantry Bay, filled with treasures collected on Grand Tours — where revolution, aristocratic elegance, and wild West Cork beauty converge.
history: Built in the early 1700s, purchased by Richard White in 1739 and owned by the White family (Earls of Bantry) ever since. Richard White was elevated to the peerage after warning of the failed 1796 French invasion fleet in Bantry Bay. The 2nd Earl filled the house with art and tapestries from European travels. Still privately occupied and open to visitors.

name: Ballinskelligs Priory
description: Atmospheric Augustinian priory ruins on the wild Kerry coast, where medieval monks lived at the edge of the known world.
history: Ballinskelligs Priory on the coast of the Iveragh Peninsula, County Kerry, was an Augustinian foundation associated with the monks of Skellig Michael who moved ashore in the 12th century. The ruins stand on a beach exposed to the Atlantic. The monks likely moved from the remote Skellig rock seeking more sustainable living conditions while maintaining their spiritual life.

name: Staigue Fort
description: One of the finest and largest stone forts in Ireland, a massive drystone circular enclosure in the wild mountains of the Iveragh Peninsula.
history: Staigue Fort near Castlecove on the Iveragh Peninsula, County Kerry, is one of the finest stone ringforts in Ireland. The massive circular drystone wall stands up to 5.5 metres high and 4 metres thick, with an interior stairway system. Dating to the Iron Age or early Christian period, it was likely a royal or ceremonial site. The spectacular mountain setting adds to its impact.

name: Castle Hyde
description: A magnificent Georgian mansion on the banks of the River Blackwater, childhood home of Douglas Hyde, first President of Ireland.
history: Castle Hyde near Fermoy, County Cork, was the childhood home of Douglas Hyde (1860-1949), founder of the Gaelic League and first President of Ireland. The elegant Georgian mansion overlooks the River Blackwater, often called the 'Irish Rhine.' The estate was later purchased by Michael Flatley of Riverdance fame.

name: Glanworth Castle
description: A dramatic medieval fortress spanning the River Funshion in north Cork, where the oldest stone bridge in Ireland still carries traffic beneath castle walls.
history: Glanworth Castle in north Cork stands dramatically above the River Funshion. The castle was built by the Roche family (Lords of Fermoy) in the 13th century. Nearby is what is claimed to be the oldest bridge in Ireland, a 13th-century stone structure still in use. The castle ruins, bridge, and Dominican friary create a remarkable medieval ensemble.

name: James Fort
description: A massive star-shaped artillery fort guarding Kinsale Harbour, built after the famous 1601 Battle of Kinsale that changed the course of Irish history.
history: James Fort on the eastern shore of Kinsale Harbour, County Cork, was built in 1602 following the Battle of Kinsale (1601)—the defeat that ended Gaelic Ireland's last major military resistance. The substantial star-shaped fortification was later superseded by Charles Fort across the harbour but remains an impressive military monument with fine harbour views.

name: Puxley Mansion
description: The dramatic ruined shell of a copper baron's mansion on the Beara Peninsula, inspiration for Daphne du Maurier's 'Hungry Hill.'
history: Puxley Mansion near Castletownbere on the Beara Peninsula, County Cork, was built by the Puxley family who made their fortune from copper mining in the Allihies mines. The grand Gothic Revival mansion inspired Daphne du Maurier's novel 'Hungry Hill.' Burned during the War of Independence in 1921, the spectacular ruin overlooks Dunboy Bay.

name: Croom Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the Limerick village of Croom, on the banks of the River Maigue where the famous Maigue Poets once held literary courts.
history: Croom Castle on the River Maigue in County Limerick dates to the medieval period. The village is famous as the home of the Maigue Poets, a circle of 18th-century Irish-language poets including Sean O Tuama and Aindrias Mac Craith who kept the bardic tradition alive during the penal era.

name: Lough Gur Visitor Centre
description: An interpretive centre for one of Ireland's most important archaeological landscapes, where Stone Age and Bronze Age monuments cluster around a magical lake.
history: Lough Gur in County Limerick is one of the most important archaeological sites in Ireland, with monuments spanning 6,000 years from the Neolithic to the medieval period. The Grange Stone Circle, Ireland's largest, stands nearby. The visitor centre interprets the extraordinary concentration of tombs, standing stones, crannogs, and medieval castles around this enchanted lake.

name: Oola Castle
description: A tower house in the Limerick-Tipperary borderlands, near the historic pass where the medieval dioceses of Cashel and Emly met.
history: Oola Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Oola on the Limerick-Tipperary border. The area was on the boundary between the medieval dioceses of Cashel and Emly. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the fertile agricultural lands of the Golden Vale.

name: Hospital Church
description: Medieval church and Knights Hospitaller preceptory remains in the Limerick village of Hospital, named for the medieval military order.
history: The village of Hospital in County Limerick takes its name from the Knights Hospitaller who established a preceptory here in the medieval period. The church ruins preserve fine medieval architectural details. The Knights Hospitaller (Order of St John of Jerusalem) held extensive properties across Ireland.

name: Islandmore Castle Killarney
description: A castle ruin on one of the islands in the Lakes of Killarney, accessible only by boat across the legendary waters.
history: Islandmore Castle on an island in the Lakes of Killarney, County Kerry, dates to the medieval period. The Killarney lakes and their islands have been inhabited since prehistoric times. The castle adds medieval heritage to the lakes' already extraordinary natural beauty.

name: Kilmalkedar Church
description: A remarkable Romanesque church on the Dingle Peninsula, where an alphabet stone, sundial, and ogham stones chart the evolution of writing in Ireland.
history: Kilmalkedar Church on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry, is a fine 12th-century Romanesque church. The site preserves an alphabet stone, a medieval sundial, ogham stones, and a cross-inscribed pillar, together charting the history of writing in Ireland from ogham to Latin script. The church was part of the medieval pilgrim route to Mount Brandon.

name: Reask Monastic Site
description: A tiny early Christian settlement on the Dingle Peninsula, where carved pillar stones bear some of the earliest Christian symbols in Ireland.
history: Reask Monastic Site on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry, is an early Christian settlement preserving decorated pillar stones with some of the earliest Christian carvings in Ireland. The small enclosure contains the remains of beehive huts, cross-slabs, and a beautifully carved pillar stone. The site exemplifies the hermitic tradition of early Irish Christianity.

name: Banna Strand Tower
description: A signal tower above the Kerry beach where Roger Casement landed from a German submarine in 1916, three days before the Easter Rising.
history: Banna Strand in County Kerry is famous as the landing place of Sir Roger Casement from a German U-boat on Good Friday 1916, three days before the Easter Rising. He was arrested nearby and later executed for treason. A memorial and signal tower mark this historic site on the long sandy beach.

name: Ballyclogh Castle Cork
description: A well-preserved tower house near Mallow in north Cork, standing in the rich agricultural land of the Blackwater Valley.
history: Ballyclogh Castle near Mallow in County Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house in the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century.

name: Blackwater Castle
description: A restored castle on the banks of the River Blackwater near Castletownroche, now a luxury venue for weddings and events.
history: Blackwater Castle near Castletownroche, County Cork, stands on the banks of the River Blackwater. The castle has medieval origins and has been restored as a luxury event and wedding venue. The Blackwater, often called the Irish Rhine, is one of Ireland's most scenic rivers.

name: Castlepook Cave Castle
description: Castle ruins near the significant Castlepook Cave in north Cork, where Ice Age animal remains were discovered.
history: Castlepook Castle stands near Castlepook Cave in north Cork, an important archaeological site where bones of brown bears, mammoths, and other Pleistocene fauna were discovered, some dating back 40,000 years.

name: Doneraile Court
description: A grand Georgian mansion set in the only surviving deer park in Ireland, where Edmund Spenser once walked and Canon Sheehan wrote.
history: Doneraile Court near Mallow, County Cork, is a Georgian mansion set in Doneraile Park, the only surviving deer park in Ireland. The estate has connections to Edmund Spenser (nearby Kilcolman Castle), Canon Sheehan (the novelist), and Elizabeth Aldworth (the first woman Freemason). The park features 166 hectares of parkland with herds of deer.

name: Bourchier Castle Lough Gur
description: A tower house on the shores of Lough Gur in Limerick, within one of Ireland's most archaeologically rich landscapes.
history: Bourchier Castle on the shores of Lough Gur, County Limerick, dates to the 15th century. Lough Gur is surrounded by one of the densest concentrations of archaeological monuments in Ireland, spanning from the Neolithic to the medieval period.

name: Cappagh Castle Limerick
description: A tower house in the Limerick countryside, part of the network of medieval fortifications in the former Desmond lordship.
history: Cappagh Castle in County Limerick is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th or 16th century.

name: Kilmallock Friary
description: Extensive Dominican friary ruins in one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval walled towns, the former capital of the Earls of Desmond.
history: Kilmallock Dominican Friary in County Limerick was founded in 1291 and features extensive well-preserved ruins. Kilmallock was the medieval capital of the Earls of Desmond and retains remarkable medieval heritage including town walls, tower houses, and religious buildings. It is often called 'the Balbec of Ireland' for its rich ruins.

name: Kilmallock Castle
description: Medieval castle and town wall remains in the historic Limerick town that was the Desmond capital—Ireland's finest walled medieval town outside Dublin.
history: Kilmallock's castle and town walls are part of one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval urban landscapes. The town was the principal seat of the Earls of Desmond and retains an extraordinary collection of medieval structures.

name: Collegiate Church Kilmallock
description: An impressive medieval collegiate church in Kilmallock, one of the finest ecclesiastical buildings in Munster.
history: The Collegiate Church of SS Peter and Paul in Kilmallock, County Limerick, dates to the 13th century and is one of the finest medieval church buildings in Munster. The substantial ruins include a tower, nave, and transepts with fine architectural details.

name: Bruree Castle
description: Castle remains near the Limerick village of Bruree, boyhood home of Eamon de Valera, the dominant figure in 20th-century Irish politics.
history: Bruree in County Limerick has castle remains and is best known as the boyhood home of Eamon de Valera (1882-1975), who dominated Irish politics for over 50 years as leader of Fianna Fail, Taoiseach, and President of Ireland. A heritage centre in the old school tells his story.

name: Mungret Abbey
description: An early Christian monastic site on the outskirts of Limerick city, where two medieval churches survive from what was once a major seat of learning.
history: Mungret Abbey on the outskirts of Limerick city was an important early Christian monastery, reputedly housing 1,500 monks at its peak. Two medieval churches survive on the site. The monastery was founded in the 6th century and became one of the great seats of learning in Munster.

name: Castleconnell Castle
description: Castle remains in the pretty Limerick village of Castleconnell on the River Shannon, famous for its salmon fishing.
history: Castleconnell Castle in County Limerick stands in a scenic village on the Shannon. The village is renowned for its salmon fishing on the Shannon and its attractive riverside setting. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Creagh Castle Skibbereen
description: A tower house near Skibbereen in West Cork, in the heart of the region most devastated by the Great Famine.
history: Creagh Castle near Skibbereen, County Cork, is a medieval tower house. Skibbereen was one of the areas worst affected by the Great Famine (1845-1852) and the town's heritage centre tells this devastating story. The castle predates the Famine by centuries.

name: Lough Hyne Tower
description: A signal tower above Ireland's only saltwater lake, Lough Hyne (Lough Ine), a unique marine nature reserve in West Cork.
history: The tower above Lough Hyne (Lough Ine) near Skibbereen, County Cork, is a Napoleonic-era signal tower. Lough Hyne is Ireland's first marine nature reserve and the only saltwater lake in the country, connected to the sea by a narrow rapid. The extraordinary biodiversity of the lake has attracted scientific study since the 19th century.

name: Shanagolden Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins in the west Limerick village of Shanagolden, near the Shannon Estuary.
history: Shanagolden Friary in County Limerick was a Franciscan foundation near the Shannon Estuary. The atmospheric ruins include church and domestic building remains.

name: Ballintotis Castle
description: A tower house in east Cork, in the fertile agricultural countryside between Castlemartyr and Midleton.
history: Ballintotis Castle is a medieval tower house in east County Cork. The castle dates to the 15th century and stands in productive farmland.

name: Kilgobbin Castle Cork
description: A tower house in the Cork countryside, one of the many medieval fortifications scattered across the county.
history: Kilgobbin Castle in County Cork is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th century.

name: Ballynacorra Castle
description: A tower house near Midleton in east Cork, in the area famous for its whiskey distilling heritage.
history: Ballynacorra Castle is a medieval tower house near Midleton, County Cork, famous for Jameson Irish Whiskey. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Abbeydorney Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins in the north Kerry village of Abbeydorney, one of the most substantial medieval monastic remains in the county.
history: Abbeydorney Abbey in County Kerry was a Cistercian foundation dating to 1154. The substantial ruins include church and domestic building remains. The abbey is one of the most significant medieval monastic sites in north Kerry.

name: Castlecove Castle
description: A tower house near the village of Castlecove on the Ring of Kerry, in one of Ireland's most spectacular coastal landscapes.
history: Castlecove Castle is a medieval tower house near Castlecove on the Iveragh Peninsula (Ring of Kerry), County Kerry. The castle stands in one of Ireland's most scenic coastal landscapes, near the famous Staigue Fort.

name: Ballybunnion Castle
description: Dramatic castle ruins perched on the cliffs above Ballybunnion's famous beach and world-renowned golf links on the Kerry coast.
history: Ballybunnion Castle stands on the cliffs above Ballybunnion, County Kerry, famous for its beach and world-class golf links (Bill Clinton is a regular visitor). The castle dates to the medieval period and its cliff-edge position creates a dramatic landmark visible from the beach.

name: Abington Castle
description: A tower house in the Limerick countryside near Murroe.
history: Abington Castle is a medieval tower house in County Limerick near Murroe.

name: Ballyseedy Castle Kerry
description: A castellated mansion near Tralee that witnessed one of the most controversial events of the Irish Civil War.
history: Ballyseedy Castle near Tralee, County Kerry, is infamous for the Ballyseedy Massacre of March 1923, when Free State forces tied anti-Treaty prisoners to a landmine. The castle itself is a 19th-century castellated mansion now operating as a hotel.

name: Tralee Castle
description: Scant remains of the great Desmond castle in Kerry's county town, where the international Rose of Tralee festival draws visitors each August.
history: Tralee Castle was a principal fortress of the Earls of Desmond. Little survives of the medieval castle in Kerry's county town. Tralee is best known for the annual Rose of Tralee festival and the Kerry County Museum.

name: Innisfallen Abbey
description: Enchanting monastic ruins on an island in the Lower Lake of Killarney, where the Annals of Innisfallen were compiled over centuries.
history: Innisfallen Island in the Lower Lake of Killarney, County Kerry, preserves the ruins of a monastery founded in the 7th century. The monks here compiled the Annals of Innisfallen, one of Ireland's most important historical manuscripts, covering events from the earliest times to 1326. The island is accessible by boat from Ross Castle.

name: Castle Lough Currane
description: A castle on an island in Lough Currane near Waterville on the Ring of Kerry, in one of Ireland's finest fishing lakes.
history: Castle Lough Currane stands on an island in Lough Currane near Waterville, County Kerry. The lake is one of Ireland's premier fishing lakes, famous for sea trout. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Ardfert Friary
description: Extensive medieval friary ruins near Tralee, featuring a fine cathedral, Franciscan friary, and Romanesque architectural details.
history: Ardfert near Tralee, County Kerry, preserves an impressive complex of medieval ecclesiastical buildings including a 13th-century cathedral, a Franciscan friary, and a Romanesque church. The cathedral features fine Gothic and Romanesque architectural details. Ardfert was the seat of the medieval bishops of Kerry.

name: Rockbarton Castle
description: A tower house near Limerick city, one of the medieval fortifications in the hinterland of Ireland's third-largest city.
history: Rockbarton Castle is a medieval tower house near Limerick city.

name: Shanagolden Abbey
description: See Shanagolden Friary—Franciscan ruins in west Limerick near the Shannon Estuary.
history: Shanagolden Abbey/Friary in County Limerick is a medieval religious house near the Shannon Estuary.
